  • Is No Response a data collection artifact?
  • Ruling out dropout

Appendix: Investigation of ‘No Response’ urban/rural classification and positive outcome gap

Author

Cristin Young

Published

March 2, 2026

Note

This report was generated using AI under general human direction. At the time of generation, the contents have not been comprehensively reviewed by a human analyst.

Background

The urban_rural field in the EInsight data captures whether a client’s service location is classified as urban or rural. In addition to “Urban” and “Rural,” a large share of records carry the value “No Response for Urban Or Rural Selected” (referred to throughout as No Response). This appendix documents a systematic investigation of whether excluding No Response clients from urban/rural comparisons is defensible, and what the No Response group represents.

The motivating observation was a meaningful gap in positive outcome rates: No Response clients achieved positive outcomes at a lower rate than either Urban or Rural clients. This appendix traces what was and was not ruled out as an explanation for that gap.

Data and setup

Show code 
library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
library(janitor)
library(broom)
library(ggrepel)
Show code 
all_data <- read_csv(
  "/Users/cristin/CWS Dropbox/Cristin Young/CYBHI Project/Rebecca and Tristan and Cristin/EInsight Data/demographic_discharge_clean.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

discharge_data <- read_csv(
  "/Users/cristin/CWS Dropbox/Cristin Young/CYBHI Project/Rebecca and Tristan and Cristin/EInsight Data/discharge_data_clean.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
Show code 
data201 <- all_data |>
  filter(
    !is.na(location),
    !is.na(pre_score),
    !is.na(post_score),
    !is.na(reason_for_discharge)
  ) |>
  mutate(positive_outcomes = factor(positive_outcomes, levels = c("No", "Yes"))) |>
  filter(intervention_start <= "2025-11-30")
Show code 
spss <- read_spss(
  "/Users/cristin/CWS Dropbox/Cristin Young/CYBHI Project/Data/MASTER Grantee Files/Quant Team File/MasterGranteeSpreadsheet_forBHESQuantTeam_1-20-26_NEW!.sav"
) |>
  zap_missing() |>
  mutate(across(where(is.labelled), as.character)) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.character), ~ na_if(str_trim(.x), ""))) |>
  rename_with(~ gsub("^@", "", .x)) |>
  clean_names() |>
  rename_with(~ gsub("^x", "", .x))

data201_spss <- left_join(
  data201, spss,
  by = c("provider" = "1a_grantee_provider_name", "provider_id" = "2grantee_provider_id")
) |>
  distinct(client_system_id, .keep_all = TRUE) |>
  rename(region = `8c_regions`)
Show code 
# Subset to Urban, Rural, and No Response
nr_vs_other <- data201_spss |>
  filter(!is.na(positive_outcomes)) |>
  mutate(
    ur_group = case_when(
      urban_rural == "No Response for Urban Or Rural Selected" ~ "No Response",
      urban_rural %in% c("Urban", "Rural")                    ~ urban_rural,
      TRUE                                                     ~ NA_character_
    )
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(ur_group))

# Assessment tools with >= 50 total records
tools_to_keep <- nr_vs_other |>
  filter(!is.na(trimmed_pre_assessment_name)) |>
  count(trimmed_pre_assessment_name) |>
  filter(n >= 50) |>
  pull(trimmed_pre_assessment_name)

# Providers with a mix of No Response and Urban/Rural records (n >= 10)
provider_response <- nr_vs_other |>
  group_by(provider) |>
  summarise(
    total           = n(),
    n_no_response   = sum(ur_group == "No Response"),
    pct_no_response = n_no_response / total,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(total >= 10)

mixed_providers <- provider_response |>
  filter(pct_no_response > 0, pct_no_response < 1) |>
  pull(provider)

Overall outcome rates by urban/rural group

The first step was to establish the size of the outcome gap across the three groups, restricted to clients with valid outcome data.

Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  group_by(ur_group) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    pct_positive = round(100 * mean(positive_outcomes == "Yes"), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(n)) |>
  rename(
    `Urban/rural group`  = ur_group,
    `N`                  = n,
    `% positive outcome` = pct_positive
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Positive outcome rates by urban/rural classification")

No Response clients (n = 4133) had a notably lower positive outcome rate than Urban or Rural clients. The gap between No Response and Urban is approximately 6.9 percentage points.

Is No Response a data collection artifact?

Before interpreting the outcome gap substantively, it was important to establish whether No Response is a meaningful population category or simply a reflection of inconsistent data entry.

Show code 
provider_response |>
  mutate(pattern = case_when(
    pct_no_response == 1 ~ "100% No Response",
    pct_no_response == 0 ~ "0% No Response (all Urban/Rural)",
    TRUE                 ~ "Mixed"
  )) |>
  count(pattern) |>
  rename(`Response pattern` = pattern, `Number of providers` = n) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Provider-level urban/rural response patterns (providers with n ≥ 10)")

Roughly a quarter of qualifying providers (n = 24) have 100% No Response — they never completed the urban/rural field. An equal number reported Urban or Rural for every client. The remaining 44 providers are mixed, enabling within-provider comparisons.

Additionally, the “Statewide” primary county accounts for the majority of No Response records: these are grantees operating across the state without a fixed county location, for whom urban/rural designation is inherently inapplicable.

Conclusion: No Response is partly a data collection artifact. However, because nearly half of providers show mixed patterns, the outcome gap cannot be dismissed as purely a reporting issue. Subsequent analyses focus on the mixed-provider subset where within-provider comparison is possible.

Ruling out dropout

One straightforward explanation for lower positive outcomes is higher dropout — clients who never complete treatment cannot show score improvement. Discharge reason distributions were compared across urban/rural groups within mixed providers.

Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  filter(provider %in% mixed_providers) |>
  group_by(ur_group, reason_for_discharge) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(ur_group) |>
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(ur_group, reason_for_discharge, pct) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = ur_group, values_from = pct) |>
  rename(`Reason for discharge` = reason_for_discharge) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Discharge reason distribution by urban/rural group (mixed providers, %)")

Dropout rates (withdrawal, no contact) are similar across groups. Mutually agreed cessation — the successful completion category — is slightly lower for No Response (92%) than Urban (95%) or Rural (97%), but the difference is modest.

To confirm that the outcome gap persists even among successful completers, positive outcome rates were compared within the mutually agreed cessation group:

Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  filter(
    provider %in% mixed_providers,
    reason_for_discharge == "Mutually agreed cessation of treatment (Successful Completion)"
  ) |>
  group_by(ur_group) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    pct_positive = round(100 * mean(positive_outcomes == "Yes"), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  rename(
    `Urban/rural group`  = ur_group,
    `N`                  = n,
    `% positive outcome` = pct_positive
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Positive outcome rates among successful completers (mixed providers)")

Conclusion: The outcome gap is not driven by differential dropout. No Response clients who complete treatment still show a lower rate of score improvement than Urban completers (~59% vs. ~64%). Subsequent analyses focus on this score improvement gap.

Outcome gap by assessment tool

The score improvement gap may not be uniform across assessment tools. Among successful completers in mixed providers, positive outcome rates were compared by tool.

Show code 
tool_gap <- nr_vs_other |>
  filter(
    provider %in% mixed_providers,
    reason_for_discharge == "Mutually agreed cessation of treatment (Successful Completion)",
    trimmed_pre_assessment_name %in% tools_to_keep
  ) |>
  group_by(trimmed_pre_assessment_name, ur_group) |>
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    pct_positive = round(100 * mean(positive_outcomes == "Yes"), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = ur_group, values_from = c(n, pct_positive)) |>
  clean_names() |>
  filter(!is.na(pct_positive_no_response), !is.na(pct_positive_urban)) |>
  mutate(
    gap  = pct_positive_no_response - pct_positive_urban,
    tool = fct_reorder(trimmed_pre_assessment_name, gap)
  )

tool_gap |>
  mutate(
    label_nr    = paste0(pct_positive_no_response, "%\n(n=", n_no_response, ")"),
    label_urban = paste0(pct_positive_urban,        "%\n(n=", n_urban,       ")")
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(pct_positive_no_response, pct_positive_urban),
    names_to  = "group",
    values_to = "pct_positive"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    group = if_else(group == "pct_positive_no_response", "No Response", "Urban"),
    label = if_else(group == "No Response", label_nr, label_urban)
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pct_positive, y = tool, color = group, group = tool)) +
  geom_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label),
    size = 2.8, lineheight = 0.85, vjust = -0.9, show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("No Response" = "#4A90D9", "Urban" = "#BBDB49"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 110), labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(x = "% Positive outcomes", y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor   = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
    legend.position    = "top"
  )

Conclusion: The gap is concentrated in PHQ-9 (No Response 39% vs. Urban 67%, −28 pp) and YOQ (60% vs. 82%, −22 pp). SDQ and PSS show near-zero gaps. Because PHQ-9 and YOQ are self-report symptom measures while SDQ and PSS are caregiver-report or parenting stress instruments, this pattern suggested the gap might reflect higher symptom severity at intake for No Response clients — which was tested in Section 10.

Demographic profile of No Response clients

To assess whether No Response clients are a harder-to-reach population, demographic variables were compared across urban/rural groups within mixed providers.

Show code 
compare_demo <- function(var) {
  nr_vs_other |>
    filter(provider %in% mixed_providers) |>
    group_by(ur_group, value = .data[[var]]) |>
    summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
    group_by(ur_group) |>
    mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1)) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(variable = var)
}

map(c("insurance_status", "primary_language", "housing_status"), compare_demo) |>
  list_rbind() |>
  select(variable, ur_group, value, pct) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = ur_group, values_from = pct) |>
  mutate(gap_nr_vs_urban = round(`No Response` - Urban, 1)) |>
  arrange(variable, desc(abs(gap_nr_vs_urban))) |>
  filter(!is.na(gap_nr_vs_urban)) |>
  rename(
    Variable      = variable,
    Category      = value,
    `Gap (NR − Urban)` = gap_nr_vs_urban
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Demographic distribution by urban/rural group (mixed providers, %)")

Notable differences:

  • Housing: No Response clients have a much higher rate of “Not Applicable” (21% vs. 1% Urban), suggesting a distinct program type rather than housing instability.
  • Insurance: No Response clients have lower Medi-Cal rates (30% vs. 49% Urban) and higher “Not Applicable” and “Don’t Know” rates, again pointing to program-type differences rather than economic disadvantage.
  • Language: No Response clients are less likely to be Spanish-speaking (18% vs. 35% Urban) and more likely to speak English (74% vs. 61%), inconsistent with a harder-to-reach immigrant population explanation.

Conclusion: The demographic profile does not support the hypothesis that No Response clients are a more disadvantaged or harder-to-reach population. The pattern of elevated “Not Applicable” responses on both housing and insurance suggests that No Response providers serve structurally different client types.

Client type and age

The “Not Applicable” pattern raised the question of whether No Response clients differ in client type (primary client vs. family member/caregiver reporter).

Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  filter(provider %in% mixed_providers) |>
  mutate(age = as.numeric(age_at_start_of_treatment_in_years)) |>
  group_by(ur_group) |>
  summarise(
    n                  = n(),
    median_age         = median(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_family_member  = round(100 * mean(primary_family_client == "Family Member", na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  rename(
    `Urban/rural group` = ur_group,
    `N`                 = n,
    `Median age (years)` = median_age,
    `% Family Member reporters` = pct_family_member
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Age and client type by urban/rural group (mixed providers)")

Urban clients are substantially more likely to be Family Member (caregiver) reporters (19%) compared to No Response (4%) and Rural (4%) clients. Age distributions are similar across groups.

Logistic regression: independent predictors of positive outcomes

To assess whether client type independently predicts outcomes after accounting for urban/rural group, a logistic regression was fitted on the full dataset (not restricted to mixed providers).

Show code 
model_data <- data201_spss |>
  filter(
    !is.na(positive_outcomes),
    !is.na(primary_family_client),
    urban_rural %in% c("Urban", "Rural", "No Response for Urban Or Rural Selected")
  ) |>
  mutate(
    outcome_bin = as.integer(positive_outcomes == "Yes"),
    ur_group    = if_else(
      urban_rural == "No Response for Urban Or Rural Selected",
      "No Response", urban_rural
    ),
    ur_group    = factor(ur_group, levels = c("Urban", "Rural", "No Response")),
    client_type = factor(primary_family_client, levels = c("Primary Client", "Family Member"))
  )

fit <- glm(outcome_bin ~ client_type + ur_group, data = model_data, family = binomial)

tidy(fit, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(across(c(estimate, conf.low, conf.high), \(x) round(x, 2)),
         p.value = round(p.value, 3)) |>
  rename(
    Term        = term,
    OR          = estimate,
    `95% CI low`  = conf.low,
    `95% CI high` = conf.high,
    `p-value`   = p.value
  ) |>
  select(-statistic, -std.error) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Logistic regression: positive outcomes predicted by client type and urban/rural group")

Both predictors are independently significant. Family Member reporters have 1.32× the odds of a positive outcome compared to Primary Clients (95% CI: 1.07–1.64, p = 0.009). However, the Rural advantage (OR = 1.9) and No Response disadvantage (OR = 0.78) both persist after adjustment, indicating that client type alone does not fully explain the urban/rural gaps.

Baseline severity on PHQ-9 and YOQ

Given that the outcome gap was concentrated in PHQ-9 and YOQ (see above), a plausible explanation was that No Response clients present with higher symptom severity at intake, leaving less room for detectable improvement.

Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  filter(
    provider %in% mixed_providers,
    trimmed_pre_assessment_name %in% c("PHQ-9", "YOQ"),
    ur_group %in% c("No Response", "Urban")
  ) |>
  mutate(pre_score = as.numeric(pre_score)) |>
  group_by(trimmed_pre_assessment_name, ur_group) |>
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    mean_pre   = round(mean(pre_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    median_pre = round(median(pre_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    sd_pre     = round(sd(pre_score, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    .groups    = "drop"
  ) |>
  rename(
    Tool       = trimmed_pre_assessment_name,
    Group      = ur_group,
    N          = n,
    Mean       = mean_pre,
    Median     = median_pre,
    SD         = sd_pre
  ) |>
  knitr::kable(caption = "Baseline (pre) score summary for PHQ-9 and YOQ by urban/rural group (mixed providers)")
Show code 
nr_vs_other |>
  filter(
    provider %in% mixed_providers,
    trimmed_pre_assessment_name %in% c("PHQ-9", "YOQ"),
    ur_group %in% c("No Response", "Urban")
  ) |>
  mutate(pre_score = as.numeric(pre_score)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = pre_score, fill = ur_group)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ trimmed_pre_assessment_name, scales = "free") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("No Response" = "#4A90D9", "Urban" = "#BBDB49"),
    name   = NULL
  ) +
  labs(x = "Pre-score", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    legend.position  = "top"
  )

Conclusion: The baseline severity hypothesis is not supported. For PHQ-9, No Response clients have lower average pre-scores than Urban clients (mean 6.2 vs. 9.9), suggesting a potential floor effect — clients starting near the minimum of the scale have less room to achieve a qualifying improvement. For YOQ, distributions are nearly identical. High baseline severity does not explain the gap.

Intervention type as underlying driver

Given that SDQ and YOQ are used almost exclusively with Family Member reporters, intervention type could be a confound — certain interventions may both attract Family Member reporters and produce better outcomes. To test this, outcome rates and Family Member proportions were examined across the most common interventions.

Show code 
top_interventions <- data201_spss |>
  filter(!is.na(positive_outcomes), !is.na(intervention_name)) |>
  count(intervention_name) |>
  filter(n >= 50) |>
  pull(intervention_name)

intervention_summary <- data201_spss |>
  filter(
    !is.na(positive_outcomes),
    intervention_name %in% top_interventions,
    trimmed_pre_assessment_name %in% tools_to_keep
  ) |>
  group_by(intervention_name) |>
  summarise(
    n              = n(),
    pct_family_mbr = round(100 * mean(primary_family_client == "Family Member", na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    pct_positive   = round(100 * mean(positive_outcomes == "Yes"), 1),
    top_tool       = names(sort(table(trimmed_pre_assessment_name), decreasing = TRUE))[1],
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(intervention_summary,
       aes(x = pct_family_mbr, y = pct_positive, color = top_tool, size = n)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = str_wrap(intervention_name, 25)),
    size = 2.5, color = "grey30", lineheight = 0.85, max.overlaps = 15
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 10), name = "n") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Primary tool") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = \(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    x = "% Family Member reporters",
    y = "% Positive outcomes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

NOMS is the primary tool for nearly all interventions, so tool selection does not vary meaningfully by intervention type. There is no consistent positive relationship between Family Member reporter rate and positive outcomes across interventions. The Promotora Peer Support Model (74% Family Members, 91% outcomes) is an outlier attributable to that intervention’s specific model rather than reporter type in general.

Conclusion: Intervention type does not appear to drive the Family Member advantage through tool or reporter-type selection. The individual-level Family Member effect identified in Section 9 appears to reflect something about reporter engagement or family responsiveness rather than which intervention was delivered.

Summary of findings

The table below summarises each candidate explanation for the No Response outcome gap and its evidential status.

Candidate explanation Verdict Key evidence
Data collection artifact (systematic non-reporters) Partial 24 providers (100% No Response); Statewide county drives ~1,800 records
Higher dropout / disengagement Ruled out Discharge reason distributions similar; gap persists among completers
Greater demographic hardship Ruled out No Response clients are less likely to be Spanish-speaking; lower Medi-Cal; more “Not Applicable”
Higher baseline symptom severity Ruled out PHQ-9 No Response clients start lower, not higher; YOQ distributions nearly identical
Client type (Family Member vs. Primary Client) Partial contributor Family Members have OR = 1.32 for positive outcomes; Urban clients have more Family Members (19% vs. 4%). Does not fully explain gap.
Intervention or tool type Ruled out NOMS dominates across interventions; no clean intervention-level pattern
Floor effect on PHQ-9 Plausible, untested No Response PHQ-9 clients start at lower scores with less room to improve
Provider-level factors Plausible, untested Within mixed providers, gap is consistent but origin unclear

Open questions

The following questions were not resolved by this analysis and represent natural next steps:

  1. Floor effect on PHQ-9: If No Response clients start near the minimum of the PHQ-9 scale, the positive outcome threshold (requiring a qualifying score decrease) may rarely be met regardless of clinical progress. Examining the distribution of pre-scores relative to the scoring threshold would clarify this.

  2. Provider-level factors: Even within mixed providers, No Response clients have systematically worse outcomes. Understanding what distinguishes these clients within a provider — whether it is a specific program strand, referral pathway, or intake process — would require provider-level consultation.

  3. Analytic treatment: Given the findings above, we recommend against pooling No Response clients with Urban or Rural in location-based analyses. The most defensible approach is either (a) to exclude No Response clients from urban/rural comparisons entirely, with a note about the systematic non-reporters, or (b) to treat No Response as a separate category with appropriate caveats about its composition.
